title: Bernoullis Principle
summary: Bernoulli's principle states that in fluid dynamics, an increase in fluid speed occurs with a decrease in pressure. Published by Daniel Bernoulli in 1738, it is derived from conservation of energy.

facts.text: Bernoulli's principle states that for a fluid flowing horizontally, an increase in speed occurs simultaneously with a decrease in pressure.
The principle is named after Swiss mathematician and physicist Daniel Bernoulli, who published it in his book Hydrodynamica in 1738.
Leonhard Euler derived Bernoulli's equation in its usual form in 1752.
Bernoulli's principle can be derived from the principle of conservation of energy.
Bernoulli's equation in its original form is valid only for incompressible flow; Bernoulli performed his experiments on liquids.
In a steady flow, the sum of kinetic energy, potential energy and internal energy remains constant at all points free of viscous forces.

title: Boyles Law
summary: Boyle's law states that the absolute pressure of a confined ideal gas is inversely proportional to its volume at constant temperature. Published by Robert Boyle in 1662.

facts.text: Boyle's law states that the absolute pressure of a confined ideal gas is inversely proportional to its volume when temperature and amount of gas remain unchanged.
Robert Boyle published his law in 1662; the relationship had first been noted by Richard Towneley and Henry Power.
Robert Hooke, Boyle's assistant, built the experimental apparatus for Boyle's gas experiments.
French physicist Edme Mariotte independently discovered the same law in 1679; it is sometimes called the Boyle–Mariotte law.
Boyle used a closed J-shaped tube with mercury to demonstrate that pressure and volume are inversely proportional.
When volume is halved, pressure is doubled; when volume is doubled, pressure is halved (at constant temperature).
Boyle's law is used to explain human breathing: increasing lung volume lowers air pressure inside, causing inhalation.
Mechanical engineering is one of the oldest and broadest of the engineering branches.
Mechanical engineering emerged as a field during the Industrial Revolution in Europe in the 18th century.
The six classic simple machines were known in the ancient Near East; the wedge and inclined plane (ramp) were known since prehistoric times.
The lever mechanism first appeared around 5,000 years ago in the Near East, where it was used in a simple balance scale.
The shadoof, the first crane machine, appeared in Mesopotamia circa 3000 BC; it used a lever to lift water.
The earliest evidence of pulleys dates back to Mesopotamia in the early 2nd millennium BC.
The Saqiyah water-wheel was developed in the Kingdom of Kush during the 4th century BC and relied on animal power.
Core areas of mechanical engineering include mechanics, dynamics, thermodynamics, materials science, design, structural analysis, and electricity.
Mechanical engineers use tools such as computer-aided design (CAD), computer-aided manufacturing (CAM), and computer-aided engineering (CAE).
In the United States, most undergraduate mechanical engineering programs are accredited by the Accreditation Board for Engineering and Technology (ABET).

Mechanical engineering programs typically take four to five years of study and can result in a Bachelor of Engineering (B.Eng.), Bachelor of Science (B.Sc.), or Bachelor of Technology (B.Tech.) degree.
In Spain, Portugal, and most of South America the formal degree title is 'Mechanical Engineer' and coursework spans five to six years.
In Italy, becoming a qualified engineer requires passing a state exam after completing five years of mechanical engineering coursework.
Mechanical engineers research, design, develop, build, and test mechanical and thermal devices, including tools, engines, and machines.
Mechanical engineering combines engineering physics and mathematics principles with materials science to design, analyze, manufacture, and maintain mechanical systems.
Mesopotamian civilization is credited by several sources with the invention of the wheel, though some recent sources suggest independent invention in both Mesopotamia and Eastern Europe.

Mechanical engineers design and analyze manufacturing plants, industrial equipment, heating and cooling systems, transport systems, motor vehicles, aircraft, watercraft, robotics, and medical devices.

title: Simple Machine
summary: A simple machine is a mechanical device that changes the direction or magnitude of a force using mechanical advantage. The six classical simple machines are lever, wheel and axle, pulley, inclined plane, wedge, and screw.

facts.text: The six classical simple machines are: lever, wheel and axle, pulley, inclined plane, wedge, and screw.
The idea of a simple machine originated with the Greek philosopher Archimedes around the 3rd century BC, who studied the lever, pulley, and screw.
Heron of Alexandria (c. 10–75 AD) listed five mechanisms that can 'set a load in motion': lever, windlass, pulley, wedge, and screw.
The ratio of the output force to the applied force is called the mechanical advantage.
The mechanical advantage of a compound machine is the product of the mechanical advantages of its component simple machines.
Archimedes said: 'Give me a place to stand on, and I will move the Earth' — expressing that force amplification via mechanical advantage has no theoretical limit.
A simple machine that dissipates no energy through friction or wear is called an ideal simple machine; in that case power in equals power out.
